happy: The email arrived at 6:42 in the morning and I read the first word five times before the rest of the sentence would come into focus. Passed. Two years of flashcards on the bus, practice exams spread across the kitchen table while dinner went cold, the polite lies I told friends about why I couldn't come out. I set the phone down, picked it up, set it down again. Outside, the garbage truck was making its slow racket down the street, and I opened the window just to hear it better, because this morning even that sounded like music. I called my mother before I remembered the time difference, and she answered groggy and alarmed, and when I told her she screamed so loud the dog started barking. I made coffee afterward and drank it standing up, grinning at the toaster like an idiot, already composing the text to everyone I knew.

When the clerk slid the tray across the glass counter, Marisol recognized the ring before he finished his sentence. Her grandmother's — the little emerald with the bent prong her grandfather never got around to fixing, missing since the estate sale eight years ago. Her hands did a small involuntary dance above the tray before she trusted herself to touch it. The clerk named a price, and she paid it without negotiating, which she would laugh about later, retelling the story for the third time at Sunday dinner. Walking out, she held her fist against her chest like something might escape from it. She called her sister from the parking lot, and they both talked over each other for ten minutes, and neither of them could finish a sentence, and that was fine. That night she put it on her own hand and cooked the old Sunday recipe, humming, getting the proportions wrong.
sad: The new owners had scheduled the demolition for a Tuesday, and Daniel drove out anyway, parking across the street where the school bus used to stop. The excavator took the porch first. He had learned to ride a bike off that porch, had sat on its steps the summer his father taught him to whittle, badly, both of them bleeding from small cuts and laughing about it. Now the railings folded like paper. He didn't cry, exactly; he just found that his chest had become very heavy, and that he kept swallowing. A neighbor he half-remembered waved from her yard, and he lifted his hand in return, slowly, like it was underwater. When the second floor came down — his bedroom window, the one that stuck every July — he started the car. He drove two blocks before pulling over, sitting with his hands in his lap, the engine running, going nowhere.

I found out from the department newsletter, of all things. A two-line item: Professor Okafor, after thirty-one years, wishes everyone well. No party I'd been told of, no last seminar, no chance to stand in her doorway one more time while she peered over her glasses and dismantled my argument so gently I'd thank her for it. I walked past her office on the way home. The nameplate was already gone, a pale rectangle on the wood where it had been. Twelve years she'd answered my emails within the hour, and I had been meaning — for months I had been meaning — to write and tell her what she'd meant. I stood in the hallway holding my bag strap with both hands. The janitor's cart squeaked somewhere around the corner. I wrote the letter that night anyway and mailed it to the department, not knowing if anything would forward.
angry: The tow company's hold music looped for the eleventh time, and Priya stood in her own driveway — her own driveway — holding the citation in a fist that had crumpled it past reading. The city had painted the curb line wrong, the contractor had photographed her bumper at an angle that hid the property line, and now a man named Dale was explaining, in the tone you'd use with a child, that release fees doubled after 5 p.m. "It was on my property," she said, and heard her own voice come out low and very precise, each word bitten off clean. Dale began to repeat the policy. She hung up, walked once around the empty driveway, and kicked the recycling bin hard enough to crack it, which solved nothing and felt accurate. Then she photographed the curb, the paint, the property stake, the sign — twenty-six photos, hands shaking — and started composing the complaint.

The byline read R. Castellanos, and underneath it sat my paragraphs — my interviews, my eight months of records requests — rearranged just enough to pretend. I read it twice standing up. The mug in my hand had gone cold and I noticed I was gripping it like I meant to crush it. I'd sent him the draft in confidence. In confidence. He'd thanked me, called it promising, suggested patience. I opened my email and typed three different first sentences, each one worse, each one with more capital letters, and deleted them all because he didn't deserve the satisfaction of seeing me sputter. My jaw ached; I'd been clenching it since the second paragraph. I printed both versions, laid them side by side on the table, and began highlighting the matches in red, line after line after line, pressing hard enough to tear the page in two places.
afraid: The evacuation map refreshed every twenty minutes, and each time the red polygon had crawled closer to the ridge behind their house. Marcus stood at the window watching the horizon glow a color that horizons shouldn't be. His wife was packing the car with a controlled efficiency he couldn't match; he had picked up the photo albums twice and put them down twice, his hands not committing to anything. The air smelled like a campfire that had gotten too big for the world. When the phone shrieked with the county alert, his whole body jerked, and he stood for a second with his heart slamming before the words even loaded. Zone 4. They were Zone 5. One zone. He counted the cars in the driveway, counted the kids in the house, counted again. "Now," he said, and his voice came out thin, a string pulled too tight. They left the lights on.

The text from my own email provider said a new device had signed in from a city I've never visited, and the recovery number on file was no longer mine. I sat very still, the way you do when something in the dark might not have seen you yet. Bank. I checked the bank. Then the card, then the retirement account, my fingers cold and clumsy on the screen, mistyping my own password twice. Every app I opened felt like turning a doorknob in an empty house. Somewhere a stranger was walking through my life, reading, taking, and I couldn't see which rooms he was already in. When the laptop pinged — just a calendar reminder — I flinched hard enough to knock my water over. I unplugged the router with both hands, as if that helped, and sat in the sudden quiet listening to my own pulse tick in my ears.
calm: The departure board flipped her flight from delayed to cancelled, and around her the gate erupted — raised voices, a man slapping his boarding pass against the counter. Ines took one slow breath, watched the chaos the way you'd watch weather, and walked to the quiet end of the concourse. She rebooked on her phone in four minutes, texted her sister a new arrival time with a shrug emoji, and bought a tea. By the window, planes moved through their patient choreography. She would miss the rehearsal dinner; she would not miss the wedding. The math was fine. She took out the novel she'd been saving, found her dog-eared page, and read forty minutes without once checking the board, her breathing even, her shoulders down where shoulders belong. When the new gate was announced, she finished her paragraph first, marked the page, and walked over without hurrying. The plane would wait or it wouldn't.

The renovation next door started at eight sharp — tile saw, compressor, a radio fighting both — and Mr. Abe carried his coffee out to the garden anyway. He set the cup on the flat stone where it always went. The noise arrived in waves, and he let it pass through him like wind through a screen door, neither resisting nor following it. He worked down the row of tomatoes at his usual pace, pinching suckers, tying one slumping vine to its stake with soft twine, taking the time to do the knot properly. A worker shouted; the saw whined up and died down. Mr. Abe noticed his own breath had settled into rhythm with the watering can's slow glug. When the foreman leaned over the fence to apologize for the racket, Mr. Abe waved the apology off with the trowel and offered him a tomato. There was plenty of summer left, and the beans didn't care about noise.
desperate: Sixty days, the notice said, and Renata had spent nine of them learning that every apartment in the county now cost double what she paid. She called the eleventh listing of the morning before realizing it was the same unit as the fourth, relisted higher. Her savings would cover a deposit or a moving truck; underline or. At lunch she called her brother, who let it ring out, and then her old landlord, and then a church she hadn't attended in six years, her voice climbing half a register with each call, the words coming faster, please, anything, even short-term. She had started doing arithmetic on everything — the car she could sell, the ring, the plasma center's rates per visit. That night she refreshed the listings at 1 a.m., then 2, as if a miracle might post after midnight, her thumb moving on its own, scroll, refresh, scroll, refresh.

Day three, and Theo had stopped pretending to work. The cat had never been gone overnight before, and now the shelter's voicemail box was full and the rain was starting again. He'd papered four blocks with flyers by noon and was widening the radius, stapling with a hand that had a blister forming, calling Biscuit's name down alleys in a voice gone hoarse and strange. He crawled under his own porch with a flashlight for the third time. He offered the neighbor kid twenty dollars, then made it fifty. At dusk he was opening every can of tuna in the pantry and setting them out in a line down the back steps, a glistening runway of last resorts, and when something rustled in the hedge he lunged at it — a possum — and stood there in the wet grass, fists at his sides, scanning the dark for anything that moved.
guilty: The friend request sat on Owen's screen with a name he hadn't let himself think about in twenty years. Danny Krezek. Seventh grade. Owen had been the one with the audience, the one who invented the nickname that followed Danny down every hallway until his family moved. Owen's thumb hovered. He could still hear his own twelve-year-old laugh, that performing bray, and the memory made his ears go hot even now, alone at his kitchen table at forty-one. He'd told the story once at a dinner party, edited, with himself as a bystander, and the lie had sat in his stomach for a week. Accept, decline. What he typed instead, deleting and retyping for most of an hour, was a message that started with the words "I owe you," and even sending it, he understood that an apology this late was also a request, and that Danny owed him nothing back.

I let them schedule the hearing before I said anything, that's the part I keep returning to. The accusation memo named the paragraphs, and I sat in the integrity office nodding along to my own defense — drafted at 2 a.m., full of words like 'convergent phrasing' — while knowing exactly which browser tab I'd had open the night the paper was due. My advisor vouched for me. That was the worst of it: watching her spend her credibility on my behalf, her hand on my shoulder in the hallway after. I won. The committee apologized for the inconvenience. I walked out into the parking lot and stood by my car for a long time, keys in my hand, unable to drive anywhere, replaying her saying 'I know this student.' I've started drafting the confession email four times. Each time I get as far as 'There's something' and stop, and hate myself a little more efficiently.
proud: For six weeks Amara had trained her replacement, and today she made herself sit in the back of the conference room with her hands folded and her mouth shut. Jun ran the quarterly review alone. He fielded the CFO's ambush question — the same one that had flattened Amara her own first year — and didn't blink: pulled up the sensitivity table he'd built himself, walked the room through it, even paused for questions the way she'd drilled him. Amara caught herself sitting straighter with every slide, chin lifting, a warmth spreading through her chest that she made no effort to hide. When the meeting broke, the CFO walked past her and said, 'Good hire.' 'Good man,' she corrected. In the elevator afterward Jun started to thank her and she waved it off, but she let herself smile the whole ride down, and called her husband from the lobby just to narrate it.

My mother kept saying the gown's sleeves were too long, fussing with them, and I let her fuss because her hands needed somewhere to go. First in the family — she'd said it to the cashier at the gas station, to the hotel clerk, to a stranger in the elevator who hadn't asked. When they called my name I heard, even over the announcer, my father's single sharp whistle from the upper deck, the one he uses at ballgames. I walked the stage slowly on purpose. Four jobs between the three of us, eleven years, two languages, one night I almost quit in a parking lot in the rain — all of it walked across that stage with me, and I let it. I shook the dean's hand like an equal. Afterward, on the lawn, my mother held the diploma cover with both hands and wouldn't give it back, showing everyone, and I stood there letting her, ten feet tall.
surprised: The notification said the name twice, as if once weren't enough: Danny Krezek wants to be your friend. Mara's coffee stopped halfway to her mouth. Danny Krezek. She had not thought that name in two decades, and now it was sitting in her phone like a coin found in winter coat pocket — except wrong, impossible, because the Danny she knew had sworn, loudly, at fourteen, never to speak to any of them again. She actually looked around her empty kitchen, as if someone might confirm she was seeing it. The profile photo loaded: gray at the temples, a kayak, a golden retriever. Danny Krezek owned a kayak. Her thumb hovered while her brain performed a kind of system reboot, recataloguing twenty years of assumptions in about four seconds. "Huh," she said out loud, to no one, twice. Then she laughed — one short, involuntary bark — and sat down to look at every photo.

We'd been talking for six years, Pavel and I — forums, then chess, then voice calls during his insomnia hours, which I'd always converted from what I assumed was Eastern Europe. Last night he mentioned, mid-sentence, the taqueria on Fountain Avenue. My taqueria. The one I can see from my balcony. I made him repeat it. Then I just sat there with my mouth slightly open while the call went quiet, both of us recalculating six years of time zones that had never existed — his 'insomnia' was just evenings; my 'early riser' was just mornings; we had been four miles apart through two presidential elections and a pandemic. 'You're joking,' I kept saying, and he kept saying 'I am not joking,' in that flat way of his, and neither of us could find the next sentence. We met for coffee today at noon. He's taller than his voice. I still keep shaking my head.
anxious: The results would post to the patient portal 'by 9 a.m.,' and Dana had been awake since four, doing the things you do instead of thinking: wiped the counters twice, reorganized the junk drawer, took the trash out before it was full. Her stomach had pulled itself into a tight little knot that no breakfast was going to untie. She checked the portal at 6:15, knowing it was pointless, and again at 6:40, and made a rule for herself — once an hour — that lasted twenty minutes. Every notification chime in the house spun her head around. On the drive to work she rehearsed both conversations, the good one and the other one, her hands at perfect ten-and-two, knuckles pale. At her desk she typed the same email three times with small errors in it. When the portal finally flashed its little red dot at 8:52, she sat looking at it, unable, for a long moment, to make her finger move.

The interview list had two names on it and the other one was Sam's. We'd applied without telling each other, then confessed over beers and laughed it off, and now I'm in the hallway outside the conference room re-tucking a shirt that doesn't need it, hearing the murmur of his answers through the wall. My mouth has gone to cotton. I keep running my three prepared examples on a loop until the words start to lose their meaning, the way any word does if you repeat it enough. The door handle moves — no, it doesn't. I check my phone: two minutes since I last checked. What if they ask about the gap year. What if Sam already nailed the gap-year question. I become aware that my knee is bouncing and stop it, and thirty seconds later it's bouncing again, an engine I don't control. The door opens. Sam comes out smiling. Of course he's smiling.
loving: Every night since the diagnosis, Hal had taken over the lunches, though Ruth could still make her own and told him so. He didn't argue; he just kept doing it. He'd learned the way she liked the apple sliced thin enough to read through, the crusts left on Tuesdays only, a note tucked under the napkin — never sentimental, just facts: 'Crossword answer is OBOE. Don't let Sharon park in your spot.' Forty years and he still got up first to warm her side of the car. At night he read aloud from the seed catalog, doing the Latin names in a pompous voice to make her laugh, and when she fell asleep mid-page he'd sit a while, marking the flowers she'd pointed at, planning a spring he intended to make sure she saw. Then he'd ease the blanket up over her shoulder, the way you'd cover something the world should be more careful with.

When my son asked to move back in at twenty-nine, he stood in the doorway like a salesman expecting the door to close. I made tea and didn't ask questions he wasn't ready to answer. After he went out for the last boxes I climbed to his old room and worked quietly: aired the mattress, found the lamp he liked, the one with the green shade, and set it on the nightstand at the angle he used to keep it. I left new towels and put his grandfather's chess set on the desk, mid-game, the way he and I had abandoned it eleven years ago — his move. I heard the truck in the driveway and started dinner, the one with the cumin he always smells from the hallway. He came in, dropped a box, stood in the kitchen doorway sniffing the air, and something in his shoulders finally came down from around his ears. 'Hi, Ma.' Hi, baby. Wash up.
bored: The transfer to Records was 'temporary,' which was eleven weeks ago. Devon's job is to verify that the scanning vendor scanned what the manifest says they scanned. The manifest is always right. He has begun timing things: the fluorescent light's flicker (every 41 seconds), the mail cart (10:15, 2:40), how long he can hold his breath (a minute ten, improving). Today he read the same line of the procedures binder four times and retained nothing, his eyes sliding off the page like water off wax. He sharpened three pencils he doesn't use. He took the long way to the bathroom, then the longer way back, counting ceiling tiles — 144, same as yesterday, same as it will be tomorrow. At 3 p.m. the clock performed its daily miracle of moving backward. He checked it against his phone. It hadn't. He yawned so wide his jaw clicked, and filed that under 'events.'

Hour five at gate C7. The delay had stopped having a reason; the board just said DELAYED in a font I had memorized down to the kerning. I'd finished my book at hour two, finished my phone battery's generosity at hour three, and was now deep into the terminal's secondary entertainment: a man across the aisle eating trail mix one component at a time, raisins first. I walked to the water fountain I didn't need. I read the safety placard about jet bridges. The gate agent made an announcement consisting entirely of words that canceled each other out. I considered the carpet pattern — hexagons, why — and realized I had been considering the carpet pattern for several minutes. My yawns had developed yawns. Somewhere around hour six I caught myself reading the nutrition facts on a water bottle with real attention, twice, and understood that time had become a substance, and I was chewing it.
